texts.title: The Book of Various Heresies, Chapters 29–43: The Gnostic Schools
texts.critical_edition: Marx, F. (ed.), Corpus Scriptorum Ecclesiasticorum Latinorum 38, Vienna: Tempsky, 1898
texts.description: The first English translation of any part of Filastrius’s catalog of heresies. These fifteen chapters cover every major Gnostic school known to the late fourth-century Church: the Simonians, Basilideans, Valentinians, Marcosians, Carpocratians, and the Barbelo-Gnostics.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet XI: The Flood
texts.critical_edition: George, A. R. (ed.), The Babylonian Gilgamesh Epic, Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2003
texts.description: The oldest flood narrative in world literature, predating Genesis by over a millennium. Utnapishtim tells Gilgamesh how the gods destroyed humanity with a great flood, and how he alone was granted immortality. Contains the earliest meditation on human mortality and the question of whether divinity can be shared with mortals.

texts.title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: The Prologue of John's Gospel from the earliest complete New Testament manuscript. The foundational text for Logos theology and the starting point for the theosis debate. Contains the key variant: Codex Sinaiticus reads 'only-begotten God' (monogenes theos) where later manuscripts read 'only-begotten Son.'

texts.title: Gospel of John 10:22–39: “I Said, You Are Gods”
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: The most direct confrontation in the Gospels over Christ's divinity. Jesus declares 'I and the Father are one,' is accused of blasphemy, and responds by quoting Psalm 82: 'I said, you are gods.' The single most contested passage in the theosis debate.

texts.title: 2 Peter 1:3–4: “Partakers of the Divine Nature”
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: The most explicit theosis statement in the New Testament: 'that you may become partakers of the divine nature.' The scriptural foundation for the entire Eastern Orthodox doctrine of divinization.

texts.title: On the Incarnation, Chapter 54: “He Became Man That We Might Become God”
texts.critical_edition: Thomson, R. W. (ed.), Athanasius: Contra Gentes and De Incarnatione, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1971
texts.description: The most famous sentence in the history of the theosis doctrine. Athanasius argues that the entire purpose of the Incarnation was not merely to save humanity from sin but to transform human nature: to make the mortal immortal, the human divine.

texts.title: Gospel of John 17:20-26: The High Priestly Prayer
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: Jesus's prayer for all future believers: "that they all may be one, just as you, Father, are in me and I in you, that they also may be in us." The most explicit statement of mutual indwelling in the New Testament, extending the divine-human unity to all believers.

texts.title: Gospel of Luke 1:1-4: The Historian's Prologue
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: Luke's prologue is the earliest statement of historical methodology in Christian literature. He tells Theophilus he has investigated everything carefully from eyewitnesses — a statement of source verification that mirrors the mission of this library.

texts.title: Gospel of Mark 1:1-11: The Beginning and the Baptism
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: The opening of the earliest Gospel. No birth narrative — straight to John the Baptist and the baptism, where the heavens are torn open, the Spirit descends, and the voice declares: "You are my beloved Son." The moment the divine enters the human.

texts.title: Gospel of Matthew 5:43-48: "Be Perfect As Your Father Is Perfect"
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: The climax of the Sermon on the Mount's ethical teaching. "Be perfect (teleioi) as your heavenly Father is perfect." The word teleios means complete, whole — reaching the telos. For the theosis tradition, this is a command to become like God.

texts.title: Refutation of All Heresies, Book 6, Chapters 24–37
texts.critical_edition: Marcovich, M. (ed.), Hippolytus: Refutatio Omnium Haeresium, Berlin: De Gruyter, 1986
texts.description: Hippolytus describes Valentinian Gnostic theology in detail: the divine Pleroma, the emanation of aeons, the fall of Sophia, the creation of the material world by the Demiurge, and the divine spark trapped within humanity.

texts.title: Against Heresies, Book III, Chapter 19: "He Became What We Are"
texts.critical_edition: Rousseau, A. and Doutreleau, L. (eds.), Sources Chrétiennes 211, Paris: Cerf, 1974
texts.description: The earliest systematic articulation of the theosis doctrine. Irenaeus argues that the Word became what we are so that we might become what he is.

texts.title: Against the Valentinians
texts.critical_edition: Fredouille, J.-C. (ed.), Sources Chrétiennes 280–281, Paris: Cerf, 1980
texts.description: Tertullian’s sharp, satirical attack on Valentinian Gnosticism. Written entirely in Latin by one of the most vivid prose stylists in Christian history.

texts.title: Against Arius, Book 1
texts.critical_edition: Henry, P. and Hadot, P. (eds.), Sources Chrétiennes 68, Paris: Cerf, 1960
texts.description: A Neoplatonist philosopher who converted to Christianity in old age, Marius Victorinus brought the full apparatus of Greek philosophical thought to bear on the question of Christ’s divinity.

texts.title: Psalm 82: "I Said, You Are Gods"
texts.critical_edition: Rahlfs, A. (ed.), Septuaginta, Stuttgart: Deutsche Bibelgesellschaft, 2006
texts.description: The psalm Jesus quotes at John 10:34. God stands in the divine assembly, judges the gods, and declares: "I said, you are gods." The most important Old Testament text for the theosis debate.

texts.title: Genesis 1:26-28: "Let Us Make Man in Our Image"
texts.critical_edition: Rahlfs, A. (ed.), Septuaginta, Stuttgart: Deutsche Bibelgesellschaft, 2006
texts.description: The creation of humanity in the image (eikon) and likeness (homoiosis) of God. The distinction between image and likeness became central to the theosis tradition: image is given at creation, likeness is achieved through divinization.

texts.title: The Nicene Creed (Niceno-Constantinopolitan, 381 AD)
texts.source: Acts of the Council of Constantinople (381 AD)
texts.critical_edition: Denzinger, H. (ed.), Enchiridion Symbolorum, 43rd ed., San Francisco: Ignatius Press, 2012
texts.description: The formal resolution of the first great theological crisis. Defines Christ as homoousios (of one substance) with the Father. For the theosis tradition, the Creed is both foundation and constraint.

texts.title: Romans 8:29-30: Conformed to the Image of His Son
texts.description: Predestined to be symmorphous (conformed) to the image of the Son. The divine plan includes human transformation into Christ's likeness.

texts.title: Galatians 2:20: Christ Lives in Me
texts.description: It is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me. The most personal statement of mutual indwelling in Paul.

texts.title: Colossians 1:15-20: The Christ Hymn
texts.description: Christ as the image of the invisible God, firstborn of all creation, in whom all the fullness (pleroma) was pleased to dwell.

texts.title: Philippians 2:5-11: The Kenosis Hymn
texts.description: Though in the form (morphe) of God, he emptied himself. The descent-ascent pattern: God descends to humanity so humanity can be exalted.

texts.title: 1 John 3:1-3: We Shall Be Like Him
texts.description: When he appears we shall be like him (homoioi auto), for we shall see him as he is. The eschatological promise of transformation through vision.

texts.title: Ephesians 4:11-16: The Measure of the Fullness of Christ
texts.description: Until we all attain to the measure of the stature of the fullness (pleroma) of Christ. Maturity as participation in divine fullness.

texts.title: Hebrews 1:1-4: The Radiance of God's Glory
texts.description: The Son is the radiance (apaugasma) of God's glory and the exact imprint (charakter) of his nature (hypostasis).

texts.title: 1 Corinthians 15:42-49: The Image of the Heavenly
texts.description: As we have borne the image of the man of dust, we shall also bear the image of the man of heaven. Bodily transformation, not just spiritual.

texts.title: Galatians 4:4-7: Adoption as Sons
texts.description: God sent his Son so that we might receive adoption (huiothesia) as sons. The Spirit of the Son in our hearts, crying Abba, Father.

texts.title: Romans 8:14-17: Heirs of God, Co-Heirs with Christ
texts.description: If children, then heirs: heirs of God and co-heirs (synkleronomoi) with Christ. Shared inheritance implies shared nature.

texts.title: Exodus 3:13-15: I AM WHO I AM
texts.description: The divine name revealed to Moses. Ego eimi ho on. The ontological claim that defines the God of Israel.

texts.title: Isaiah 6:1-8: The Throne Vision
texts.description: Holy, holy, holy. The seraphim, the coal that purifies lips, the call to prophecy. The paradigmatic encounter with divine glory.

texts.title: Ezekiel 1:26-28: The Divine Throne-Chariot
texts.description: Upon the likeness of a throne, the likeness of the appearance of a man. The glory of the Lord. Foundation of the merkabah tradition.

texts.title: Daniel 7:13-14: One Like a Son of Man
texts.description: One like a son of man, coming with the clouds of heaven, receiving everlasting dominion. Jesus's preferred self-designation.

texts.title: Proverbs 8:22-31: Wisdom Before Creation
texts.description: The Lord created/possessed me at the beginning. Wisdom (Sophia) as pre-existent divine agent. Arians said created; Nicenes said begotten.

texts.title: Ecclesiastes 3:10-11: Eternity in the Heart
texts.description: He has put eternity (aion/olam) into the human heart. The claim that something infinite is placed within mortal humanity.
